revision.text:
'''竪穴式石室'''(たてあなしきせきしつ)は[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬形式である。発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。
== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 関連項目 == *[[日本の古墳一覧]] *[[古墳]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎著
{{Japanese-history-stub|たてあなしきせきしつ}} [[Category:考古学|たてあなしきせきしつ]] [[Category:日本の考古遺跡|たてあなしきせきしつ]] [[Category:古墳|たてあなしきせきしつ]] [[Category:古墳時代|たてあなしきせきしつ]]

'''竪穴式石室'''（たてあなしきせきしつ）は[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬形式である。発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。
日本最大級の竪穴式石室は、[[長野県]][[千曲市]]の[[森将軍塚古墳]]のもので、長さ 7.6m、幅 2m、高さ 2.3mである。[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html]
== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著
{{DEFAULTSORT:たてあなしきせきしつ}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

revision.comment: 竪穴式石槨、外部リンクを脚注に
revision.text:
'''竪穴式石室'''（たてあなしきせきしつ）は[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬形式である。発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。
木棺と石で築いた壁のあいだに空間があまりないので、これを石室ではなく外側の棺と解釈して'''竪穴式石槨'''と表記する場合も多い。
日本最大級の竪穴式石室は、[[長野県]][[千曲市]]の[[森将軍塚古墳]]のもので、長さ 7.6m、幅 2m、高さ 2.3mである<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html 千曲市森将軍塚古墳館 竪穴式石室]</ref>。
== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:たてあなしきせきしつ}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

'''竪穴式石室'''（たてあなしきせきしつ）は[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬施設である。
== 概要 == 発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。 竪穴式石室に割竹形木棺で埋葬され、3～4世紀代によく使用された。その基本構造は、割竹形木棺を墓壙の底に安置したあと、棺に接する部分に板状の石を重ねて行き、棺と板石の間に角礫を隙間なく詰め込んで、最後に大きな蓋石を被せる。木棺を置く場所にあらかじめ粘土を敷いたり、墓壙床全面に砂利を敷いたりそている。墓壙内に浸透してきた雨水を 排水するための暗渠排水施設を設けている石室もある。積み上げられる石の量も膨大なもので、総量数トンに達する。その石材は[[二上山]]の[[サヌカイト]]が一般的であるが、徳島県の[[吉野川]]下流域の[[結晶片岩]]もよく使われている。石室の構築には、相当な石の量が運ばれたと想定しなければいけない。
木棺と石で築いた壁のあいだに空間があまりないので、これを石室ではなく外側の棺と解釈して'''竪穴式石槨'''と表記する場合も多い。
日本最大級の竪穴式石室は、[[長野県]][[千曲市]]の[[森将軍塚古墳]]のもので、長さ 7.6m、幅 2m、高さ 2.3mである<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html 千曲市森将軍塚古墳館 竪穴式石室]</ref>。
== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:たてあなしきせきしつ}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

'''竪穴式石室'''（たてあなしきせきしつ）は[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬施設である。
== 概要 == 発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。 竪穴式石室に割竹形木棺を埋葬する方法は、3～4世紀代に流行した。 その基本構造は、割竹形木棺を墓壙の底に安置したあと、棺に接する部分に板状の石を重ねていき、棺と板石の間に角礫を隙間なく詰め込んで、最後に大きな蓋石を被せる。 木棺を置く場所にあらかじめ粘土を敷いたり、墓壙床全面に砂利を敷いたりそている。墓壙内に浸透してきた雨水を排水するための暗渠排水施設を設けている石室もある。積み上げられる石の量も膨大なもので、総量数トンに達する。その石材は[[二上山]]の[[サヌカイト]]が一般的であるが、徳島県の[[吉野川]]下流域の[[結晶片岩]]もよく使われている。石室の構築には、相当な量の石が運ばれたと想定しなければいけない。 割竹形木棺の内・外の面と石室の壁面とに[[ベンガラ]]（赤色顔料）が塗られている。棺の内側には[[朱]]（水銀朱）]]が塗られている場合もあり、ベンガラと朱の両方が用いられる場合もある。埴輪を造ることが埋葬祭祀の一部であるととられられているので、石室構築も同様に埋葬祭祀行為であったと考えられる。 木棺と石で築いた壁のあいだに空間があまりないので、これを石室ではなく外側の棺と解釈して'''竪穴式石槨'''と表記する場合も多い。
このような石室・石槨を構築するのは、首長の亡骸を棺に収め、石で覆い固め封じ込める意味があったのではないかと推測されている。
日本最大級の竪穴式石室は、[[長野県]][[千曲市]]の[[森将軍塚古墳]]のもので、長さ 7.6m、幅 2m、高さ 2.3mである<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html 千曲市森将軍塚古墳館 竪穴式石室]</ref>。
== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:たてあなしきせきしつ}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

'''竪穴式石室'''（たてあなしきせきしつ）は[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬施設である。
== 概要 == 発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。 竪穴式石室に割竹形木棺を埋葬する方法は、3～4世紀代に流行した。 その基本構造は、割竹形木棺を墓壙の底に安置したあと、棺に接する部分に板状の石を重ねていき、棺と板石の間に角礫を隙間なく詰め込んで、最後に大きな蓋石を被せる。 木棺を置く場所にあらかじめ粘土を敷いたり、墓壙床全面に砂利を敷いたりそている。墓壙内に浸透してきた雨水を排水するための暗渠排水施設を設けている石室もある。積み上げられる石の量も膨大なもので、総量数トンに達する。その石材は[[二上山]]の[[サヌカイト]]が一般的であるが、徳島県の[[吉野川]]下流域の[[結晶片岩]]もよく使われている。石室の構築には、相当な量の石が運ばれたと想定しなければいけない。 割竹形木棺の内・外の面と石室の壁面とに[[ベンガラ]]（赤色顔料）が塗られている。棺の内側には[[朱]]（水銀朱）が塗られている場合もあり、ベンガラと朱の両方が用いられる場合もある。埴輪を造ることが埋葬祭祀の一部であるととられられているので、石室構築も同様に埋葬祭祀行為であったと考えられる。 木棺と石で築いた壁のあいだに空間があまりないので、これを石室ではなく外側の棺と解釈して'''竪穴式石槨'''と表記する場合も多い。

== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:たてあなしきせきしつ}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

== 概要 == 発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。 竪穴式石室に割竹形木棺を埋葬する方法は、3～4世紀代に流行した。 その基本構造は、割竹形木棺を墓壙の底に安置したあと、棺に接する部分に板状の石を重ねていき、棺と板石の間に角礫を隙間なく詰め込んで、最後に大きな蓋石を被せる。 木棺を置く場所にあらかじめ粘土を敷いたり、墓壙床全面に砂利を敷いたりそている。墓壙内に浸透してきた雨水を排水するための暗渠排水施設を設けている石室もある。積み上げられる石の量も膨大なもので、総量数トンに達する。その石材は[[二上山]]の[[サヌカイト]]が一般的であるが、徳島県の[[吉野川]]下流域の[[結晶片岩]]もよく使われている。石室の構築には、相当な量の石が運ばれたと想定しなければいけない。 割竹形木棺の内・外の面と石室の壁面とに[[ベンガラ]]（赤色顔料）が塗られている。棺の内側には[[朱]]（水銀朱）が塗られている場合もあり、ベンガラと朱の両方が用いられる場合もある。埴輪を造ることが埋葬祭祀の一部であるととらえられているので、石室構築も同様に埋葬祭祀行為であったと考えられる。 木棺と石で築いた壁のあいだに空間があまりないので、これを石室ではなく外側の棺と解釈して'''竪穴式石槨'''と表記する場合も多い。
このような石室・石槨を構築するのは、首長の亡骸を棺に収め、石で覆い固め封じ込める意味があったのではないかと推測されている。
日本最大級の竪穴式石室は、[[長野県]][[千曲市]]の[[森将軍塚古墳]]のもので、長さ 7.6m、幅 2m、高さ 2.3mである<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html 千曲市森将軍塚古墳館 竪穴式石室]</ref>。
== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:たてあなしきせきしつ}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}

'''竪穴式石室'''（たてあなしきせきしつ）は[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬施設である。
== 概要 == 発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。竪穴式石室に[[割竹形木棺]]を埋葬する方法は、[[3世紀]]代から[[4世紀]]代にかけて流行した。
その基本構造は、割竹形木棺を墓壙の底に安置したあと、棺に接する部分に板状の石を重ねていき、棺と板石の間に角礫を隙間なく詰め込んで、最後に大きな蓋石をかぶせる。木棺を置く場所にあらかじめ[[粘土]]を敷いたり、墓壙の床全面に[[砂利]]を敷いたりしている。墓壙内に浸透してきた雨水を排水するための暗渠排水施設を設けている石室もある。積み上げられる石の量も膨大なもので、総量数トンに達する。その石材は[[二上山 (奈良県・大阪府)|二上山]]の[[サヌカイト]]が一般的であるが、徳島県の[[吉野川]]下流域の[[結晶片岩]]もよく使われている。石室の構築には、相当な量の石が運ばれたと想定しなければいけない。
割竹形木棺の内・外の面と石室の壁面とに[[弁柄|ベンガラ]]（[[赤|赤色]][[顔料]]）が塗られることが多い。棺の内側には[[朱 (顔料)|朱]]（水銀朱）が塗られている場合もあり、ベンガラと朱の両方が用いられる場合もある。埴輪を造ることが埋葬祭祀の一部であるととらえられているので、石室構築も同様に埋葬祭祀行為であったと考えられる。
木棺と石で築いた壁のあいだに空間があまりないので、これを石室ではなく外側の棺と解釈して'''竪穴式石槨'''と表記する場合も多い。
このような石室・石槨を構築するのは、首長の亡骸を棺に収め、石で覆い固め封じ込める意味があったのではないかと推測されている。
日本最大級の竪穴式石室は、[[長野県]][[千曲市]]の[[森将軍塚古墳]]のもので、長さ 7.6メートル、幅 2メートル、高さ 2.3メートルにおよぶものである<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html 千曲市森将軍塚古墳館 竪穴式石室]</ref>。
== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 例 == #雪野山古墳 - 滋賀県東近江市（旧八日市市）近江八幡市・竜王町、前期前半で全長70メートルの前方後円墳、竪穴式石室に[[舟形木棺]]を納める。
== 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著
== 脚注 == {{reflist}}

'''竪穴式石室'''（たてあなしきせきしつ）は[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬施設である。
== 概要 == 発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。竪穴式石室に[[割竹形木棺]]を埋葬する方法は、[[3世紀]]代から[[4世紀]]代にかけて流行した。
その基本構造は、割竹形木棺を墓壙の底に安置したあと、棺に接する部分に板状の石を重ねていき、棺と板石の間に角礫を隙間なく詰め込んで、最後に大きな蓋石をかぶせる。木棺を置く場所にあらかじめ[[粘土]]を敷いたり、墓壙の床全面に[[砂利]]を敷いたりしている。墓壙内に浸透してきた雨水を排水するための暗渠排水施設を設けている石室もある。積み上げられる石の量も膨大なもので、総量数トンに達する。その石材は[[二上山 (奈良県・大阪府)|二上山]]の[[サヌカイト]]が一般的であるが、徳島県の[[吉野川]]下流域の[[結晶片岩]]もよく使われている。石室の構築には、相当な量の石が運ばれたと想定しなければいけない。 木棺と石で築いた壁のあいだに空間があまりないので、これを石室ではなく外側の棺と解釈して'''竪穴式石槨'''と表記する場合も多い。 4世紀半ばから簡略化された[[粘土槨]]が普及する。この場合も、木棺を覆う空間を残さず、直接木棺を棺床の粘土と同じ粘土で包み込んだ後、墓壙を埋める。その被覆粘土は、棒状の道具で念入りに叩き締められている。
割竹形木棺の内・外の面と石室の壁面とに[[弁柄|ベンガラ]]（[[赤|赤色]][[顔料]]）が塗られることが多い。棺の内側には[[朱 (顔料)|朱]]（水銀朱）が塗られている場合もあり、ベンガラと朱の両方が用いられる場合もある。埴輪を造ることが埋葬祭祀の一部であるととらえられているので、石室構築も同様に埋葬祭祀行為であったと考えられる。 このような石室・石槨を構築するのは、密閉と防湿に配慮しており、また、首長の亡骸を棺に収め保存するとともに外敵から護るという意味もあり、さらに石で覆い固め封じ込める意味があったのではないかと推測されている。このような構築方法や埋葬の意味が5世紀以降薄れていく。
日本最大級の竪穴式石室は、[[長野県]][[千曲市]]の[[森将軍塚古墳]]のもので、長さ 7.6メートル、幅 2メートル、高さ 2.3メートルにおよぶものである<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html 千曲市森将軍塚古墳館 竪穴式石室]</ref>。
== 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。
== 例 == #雪野山古墳 - 滋賀県東近江市（旧八日市市）近江八幡市・竜王町、前期前半で全長70メートルの前方後円墳、竪穴式石室に[[舟形木棺]]を納める。
== 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]]
== 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著

上部（上）と内部（下）、[[森将軍塚古墳館]]展示。}} '''竪穴式石室'''（たてあなしきせきしつ）は、[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬施設である。 == 概要 == 発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。竪穴式石室に[[割竹形木棺]]を埋葬する方法は、[[3世紀]]代から[[4世紀]]代にかけて流行した。 その基本構造は、割竹形木棺を墓壙の底に安置したあと、棺に接する部分に板状の石を重ねていき、棺と板石の間に角礫を隙間なく詰め込んで、最後に大きな蓋石をかぶせる。木棺を置く場所にあらかじめ[[粘土]]を敷いたり、墓壙の床全面に[[砂利]]を敷いたりしている。墓壙内に浸透してきた雨水を排水するための暗渠排水施設を設けている石室もある。積み上げられる石の量も膨大なもので、総量数トンに達する。その石材は[[二上山 (奈良県・大阪府)|二上山]]の[[サヌカイト]]が一般的であるが、徳島県の[[吉野川]]下流域の[[結晶片岩]]もよく使われている。石室の構築には、相当な量の石が運ばれたと想定しなければいけない。 木棺と石で築いた壁のあいだに空間があまりないので、これを石室ではなく外側の棺と解釈して'''竪穴式石槨'''と表記する場合も多い。 4世紀半ばから簡略化された[[粘土槨]]が普及する。この場合も、木棺を覆う空間を残さず、直接木棺を棺床の粘土と同じ粘土で包み込んだ後、墓壙を埋める。その被覆粘土は、棒状の道具で念入りに叩き締められている。 割竹形木棺の内・外の面と石室の壁面とに[[弁柄|ベンガラ]]（[[赤|赤色]][[顔料]]）が塗られることが多い。棺の内側には[[朱 (顔料)|朱]]（水銀朱）が塗られている場合もあり、ベンガラと朱の両方が用いられる場合もある。埴輪を造ることが埋葬祭祀の一部であるととらえられているので、石室構築も同様に埋葬祭祀行為であったと考えられる。 このような石室・石槨を構築するのは、密閉と防湿に配慮しており、また、首長の亡骸を棺に収め保存するとともに外敵から護るという意味もあり、さらに石で覆い固め封じ込める意味があったのではないかと推測されている。このような構築方法や埋葬の意味が5世紀以降薄れていく。 日本最大級の竪穴式石室は[[長野県]][[千曲市]]の[[森将軍塚古墳]]のもので、長さ 7.6メートル、幅 2メートル、高さ 2.3メートルにおよぶ[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html 千曲市森将軍塚古墳館 竪穴式石室]。 == 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。 == 例 == #雪野山古墳 - 滋賀県東近江市（旧八日市市）近江八幡市・竜王町、前期前半で全長70メートルの前方後円墳、竪穴式石室に[[舟形木棺]]を納める。 == 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]] == 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たてあなしきせきしつ}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

上部（上）と内部（下）、[[森将軍塚古墳館]]展示。}} '''竪穴式石室'''（たてあなしきせきしつ）は、[[古墳時代]]前期から中期にかけてよく見られる[[古墳]]の埋葬施設である。 == 概要 == 発掘過程で竪穴の石室のように検出する事からその名がついた。竪穴式石室に[[割竹形木棺]]を埋葬する方法は、[[3世紀]]代から[[4世紀]]代にかけて流行した。 その基本構造は、割竹形木棺を墓壙の底に安置したあと、棺に接する部分に板状の石を重ねていき、棺と板石の間に角礫を隙間なく詰め込んで、最後に大きな蓋石をかぶせる。木棺を置く場所にあらかじめ[[粘土]]を敷いたり、墓壙の床全面に[[砂利]]を敷いたりしている。墓壙内に浸透してきた雨水を排水するための暗渠排水施設を設けている石室もある。積み上げられる石の量も膨大なもので、総量数トンに達する。その石材は[[二上山 (奈良県・大阪府)|二上山]]の[[サヌカイト]]が一般的であるが、徳島県の[[吉野川]]下流域の[[結晶片岩]]もよく使われている。石室の構築には、相当な量の石が運ばれたと想定しなければいけない。 木棺と石で築いた壁のあいだに空間があまりないので、これを石室ではなく外側の棺と解釈して'''竪穴式石槨'''と表記する場合も多い。 4世紀半ばから簡略化された[[粘土槨]]が普及する。この場合も、木棺を覆う空間を残さず、直接木棺を棺床の粘土と同じ粘土で包み込んだ後、墓壙を埋める。その被覆粘土は、棒状の道具で念入りに叩き締められている。 割竹形木棺の内・外の面と石室の壁面とに[[弁柄|ベンガラ]]（[[赤|赤色]][[顔料]]）が塗られることが多い。棺の内側には[[朱 (顔料)|朱]]（水銀朱）が塗られている場合もあり、ベンガラと朱の両方が用いられる場合もある。埴輪を造ることが埋葬祭祀の一部であるととらえられているので、石室構築も同様に埋葬祭祀行為であったと考えられる。 このような石室・石槨を構築するのは、密閉と防湿に配慮しており、また、首長の亡骸を棺に収め保存するとともに外敵から護るという意味もあり、さらに石で覆い固め封じ込める意味があったのではないかと推測されている。このような構築方法や埋葬の意味が5世紀以降薄れていく。 日本最大級の竪穴式石室は[[長野県]][[千曲市]]の[[森将軍塚古墳]]のもので、長さ 7.6メートル、幅 2メートル、高さ 2.3メートルにおよぶ[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html 千曲市森将軍塚古墳館 竪穴式石室]。 == 製作過程 == #[[墳丘]]に掘った大きな土壙のそこの中央部に粘土をひく。 #粘土の上に[[木棺]]を安置して遺骸を埋葬する。 #木棺の四方に板石ないし割石を積み上げて壁にを構成し、壁の背後の裏込めも全て石材で充填する。 #さらに四壁の上に大きな天井石数個を並べて封鎖し、その上に粘土を厚く被覆して土で埋める。 == 例 == #雪野山古墳 - 滋賀県東近江市（旧八日市市）近江八幡市・竜王町、前期前半で全長70メートルの前方後円墳、竪穴式石室に[[舟形木棺]]を納める。 == 関連項目 == *[[粘土槨]] *[[横穴式石室]] *[[古墳]] *[[日本の古墳一覧]] == 参考文献 == *『古墳とヤマト政権』文春新書 白石太一郎（[[奈良大学]]教授）著 == 脚注 == {{reflist}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たてあなしきせきしつ}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} [[Category:日本の考古学]] [[Category:遺構]] [[Category:古墳]]

revision.comment: Created page with "{{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館}}.}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial ch..."
revision.text:
{{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館}}.}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト}} from {{ill|Mt. Nijō|ja|二上山}} in the Kansai region or {{ill|crystalline schist|ja|結晶片岩}} from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengara|ja|弁柄}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|mercury vermilion|ja|朱}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- **Yukinoyama Kofun**: Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).

{{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館}}.}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト}} from {{ill|Mt. Nijō|ja|二上山}} in the Kansai region or {{ill|crystalline schist|ja|結晶片岩}} from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengara|ja|弁柄}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|mercury vermilion|ja|朱}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- **Yukinoyama Kofun**: Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==

The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengara|ja|弁柄}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|mercury vermilion|ja|朱}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- **Yukinoyama Kofun**: Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==

Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト}} from {{ill|Mt. Nijō|ja|二上山}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengara|ja|弁柄}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|mercury vermilion|ja|朱}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- **Yukinoyama Kofun**: Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==

[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館}}.}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト}} from {{ill|Mt. Nijō|ja|二上山}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengara|ja|弁柄}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|mercury vermilion|ja|朱}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}

revision.text:
[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|en}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト|en}} from {{ill|Mt. Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|en}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト|en}} from {{ill|Mt. Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.
== Footnotes == {{reflist|en}}

== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}

The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト|en}} from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).

Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.

== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト|en}} from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.
== Footnotes == {{reflist|en}}

[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|en}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト|en}} from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including {{ill|sanukite|ja|サヌカイト|en}} from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.

In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.

The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.

1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. - '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).

- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}

[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|en}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== Related Topics ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|en}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

revision.text:
[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|en}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.
== Footnotes == {{reflist|en}}

[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|en}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

revision.text:
[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|en}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (upper) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/app/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.
== Footnotes == {{reflist|en}}

[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|en}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (uqqer) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/aqq/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).

* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|en}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}} * {{ill|Kofun|ja|古墳|en}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en}}

[[Category:kk]] [[Category:completion tier 5]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical pit stone chamber]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:竪穴式石室]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]][[Category:qq]]

[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Double image stack|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|en}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (uqqer) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代|en}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|clay|ja|粘土|en}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|en}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|ja|吉野川|en}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|en}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|ja|千曲市|en}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/aqq/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|en}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
- '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi, Shiga|ja|東近江市|en}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|en}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin).

== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|ja|奈良大学|en}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.
