<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11598499]] Wikidata name: vertical stone chamber [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Multiple image|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} ([[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wari-takegata mokkan|qq=}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, [[:en:Clay|clay]] is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower [[:en:Yoshino River|Yoshino River]] basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi, Shiga]], this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower [[:en:Yoshino River|Yoshino River]] basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi, Shiga]], this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]]), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11598499]] Wikidata name: vertical stone chamber [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Multiple image|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} ([[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wari-takegata mokkan|qq=}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, [[:en:Clay|clay]] is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower [[:en:Yoshino River|Yoshino River]] basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi, Shiga]], this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11598499]] Wikidata name: vertical stone chamber [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Multiple image|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} ([[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wari-takegata mokkan|qq=}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, [[:en:Clay|clay]] is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower [[:en:Yoshino River|Yoshino River]] basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi, Shiga]], this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]]), Bungeishunju Shinsho.
== Footnotes == {{reflist|en}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11598499]] Wikidata name: vertical stone chamber [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Multiple image|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} ([[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wari-takegata mokkan|qq=}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, [[:en:Clay|clay]] is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower [[:en:Yoshino River|Yoshino River]] basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi, Shiga]], this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]]), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi, Shiga]], this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11598499]] Wikidata name: vertical stone chamber [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:19, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Multiple image|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} ([[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]]): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wari-takegata mokkan|qq=}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, [[:en:Clay|clay]] is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower [[:en:Yoshino River|Yoshino River]] basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi, Shiga]], this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]]), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, [[:en:Clay|clay]] is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or [[:en:Schist|crystalline schist]] from the lower [[:en:Yoshino River|Yoshino River]] basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in [[:en:Chikuma, Nagano|Chikuma, Nagano]], measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in [[:en:Higashiōmi|Higashiōmi, Shiga]], this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * [[:en:Kofun|Kofun]] * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, [[:en:Nara University|Nara University]]), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in [[:en:Kofun period|Kofun period]] tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}

Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wari-takegata mokkan|qq=}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|Clay|en|Clay|WD=Q42302|lt=clay}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or {{ill|Schist|en|Schist|WD=Q15315|lt=crystalline schist}} from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|en|Yoshino River|WD=Q979774|lt=Yoshino River}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma, Nagano}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|WD=Q240891|lt=Higashiōmi, Shiga}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|en|Nara University|WD=Q8190036|lt=Nara University}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.
== Footnotes == {{reflist|en}}

Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}} tombs during the early to middle periods.

[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Multiple image|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. * '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|WD=Q240891|lt=Higashiōmi, Shiga}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:竪穴式石室]][[zh:豎穴式石室]]
[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Multiple image|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}
== Overview ==
This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wari-takegata mokkan|qq=}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.
The basic structure involves placing a split-log coffin at the bottom of a burial pit, surrounding it with flat stones tightly packed with angular gravel, and covering the entire structure with large capstones. Beneath the coffin, {{ill|Clay|en|Clay|WD=Q42302|lt=clay}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or {{ill|Schist|en|Schist|WD=Q15315|lt=crystalline schist}} from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|en|Yoshino River|WD=Q979774|lt=Yoshino River}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma, Nagano}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|WD=Q240891|lt=Higashiōmi, Shiga}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|en|Nara University|WD=Q8190036|lt=Nara University}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.
== Footnotes == {{reflist|en}}

Beneath the coffin, {{ill|Clay|en|Clay|WD=Q42302|lt=clay|1=Clay}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or {{ill|Schist|en|Schist|WD=Q15315|lt=crystalline schist|1=Schist}} from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|en|Yoshino River|WD=Q979774|lt=Yoshino River|1=Yoshino River}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.
Due to minimal space between the coffin and the stone walls, these are sometimes referred to as "Tateana-shiki sekikaku" (竪穴式石槨, vertical pit-style stone sarcophagi). By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma, Nagano|1=Chikuma, Nagano}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>
== Construction Process ==
1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
== Examples ==
* '''Yukinoyama Kofun''': Located in {{ill|Higashiōmi|en|Higashiōmi|WD=Q240891|lt=Higashiōmi, Shiga|1=Higashiōmi}}, this early 4th-century keyhole-shaped tumulus measures 70 meters in total length and features a vertical pit-style stone chamber containing a {{ill|funa-gata mokkan|ja|舟形木棺|1=Funagata Mokkan|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/funa-gata mokkan|13=User:Immanuelle/Funagata Mokkan|qq=}} (boat-shaped wooden coffin). == See Also ==
* {{ill|Clay sarcophagus|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}} * {{ill|Kofun|en|Kofun|WD=Q1141225|lt=Kofun|1=Kofun}} * {{ill|List of kofun in Japan|ja|日本の古墳一覧|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of kofun in Japan|1=list of kofun|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11505809}}
== References == * *{{ill|Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja|古墳とヤマト政権|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kofun and Yamato Polity|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}* by Taichiro Shiraishi (Professor, {{ill|Nara University|en|Nara University|WD=Q8190036|lt=Nara University|1=Nara University}}), Bungeishunju Shinsho.

[[File:Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|thumb]] {{Multiple image|right|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu.JPG|Mori-shogunzuka-kofun sekishitsu (inside).JPG|200|{{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} ({{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma, Nagano|1=Chikuma, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}): Restored vertical pit-style stone chamber. Top (u11er) and interior (lower) as displayed in the {{ill|Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|ja|森将軍塚古墳館|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum|1=Mori-Shōgunzuka Museum, Chikuma City|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11539448}}.|en}} '''Tateana-shiki sekishitsu''' (竪穴式石室) refers to burial chambers commonly used in {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} tombs during the early to middle periods.
They are vertical and contrast with the horizontal {{ill|Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|ja|横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|2=ja|3=横穴式石室|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|4=zh|5=橫穴式石室|6=ko|7=굴식 돌방무덤|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|1=Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokoana-shiki sekishitsu|13=User:Immanuelle/Corridor-Style Stone Chamber|qq=}}

This burial style derives its name from the way chambers are detected during excavations, resembling vertical pits. The practice of interring {{ill|Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Waritakegata Mokkan|lt=Wari-takegata mokkan|ja|割竹形木棺|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wari-takegata mokkan|qq=}} (split-log coffins) in vertical pit-style stone chambers was prevalent from the 3rd to 4th centuries CE.

Beneath the coffin, {{ill|Clay|en|Clay|WD=Q42302|lt=clay|1=Clay}} is often laid, or the floor is lined with gravel. Some chambers include underground drainage systems to prevent water infiltration. The total weight of the stones used can amount to several tons, with common materials including [[:en:Sanukite|sanukite]] from {{ill|Mount Nijō|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Nijō|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} in the Kansai region or {{ill|Schist|en|Schist|WD=Q15315|lt=crystalline schist|1=Schist}} from the lower {{ill|Yoshino River|en|Yoshino River|WD=Q979774|lt=Yoshino River|1=Yoshino River}} basin in Tokushima Prefecture.

By the mid-4th century, simplified {{ill|clay sarcophagi|ja|粘土槨|1=Clay coffin chamber|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/clay sarcophagi|13=User:Immanuelle/Clay coffin chamber|qq=}} gained popularity. In this method, coffins are directly encased in the same clay as the burial pit floor and compacted with sticks.
The interiors and exteriors of split-log coffins and chamber walls were often coated with {{ill|bengala|ja|弁柄|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bengala|qq=}} (red ochre pigment) or {{ill|cinnabar|ja|朱|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cinnabar|1=朱|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11093510}}. The use of these pigments and the construction of burial chambers are considered to have been part of the funerary rituals. The chamber design emphasized hermetic sealing and moisture resistance to preserve the remains of leaders and protect them from external threats. Additionally, the act of encasing the deceased in stone may have held symbolic significance. Such practices and meanings began to decline after the 5th century.
The largest vertical pit-style stone chamber in Japan is found in the {{ill|Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Hanishina Kofun group|lt=Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja|森将軍塚古墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mori-shogunzuka Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 埴科古墳群}} in {{ill|Chikuma, Nagano|en|Chikuma, Nagano|WD=Q179757|lt=Chikuma, Nagano|1=Chikuma, Nagano}}, measuring 7.6 meters in length, 2 meters in width, and 2.3 meters in height.<ref>[http://www.city.chikuma.nagano.jp/a11/kofun/Sekisitu.html Mori-shogunzuka Kofun Museum - Vertical Pit-Style Stone Chamber]</ref>

1. Dig a large burial pit at the mound site and lay clay at the base. 2. Place a {{ill|mokkan|ja|木棺|1=Wooden Coffin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mokkan|qq=}} (wooden coffin) on the clay and inter the deceased. 3. Build walls around the coffin using flat or split stones, filling the gaps behind the walls with additional stones. 4. Seal the structure with large capstones, cover the top with clay, and fill the burial pit with soil.
